unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 2! This unit is all about school and learning. You will talk about your lessons, your teacher, and the subjects you study, like mathematics and grammar. You will learn the words for the supplies you use every day, such as an eraser and your books. You will find out how to get ready for a contest, write a short report, and use the library well. Best of all, you will learn to talk about things that happened in the past — what you studied, where you went, and why you enjoyed it. By the end, you will be ready to cheer for the Grammar Champions in this unit’s story!
unit.learningPath:
Start with the Vocabulary section and learn a few school words each day.
Work through the six grammar lessons, one at a time, from the simple past tense to the possessive ’s and because.
Read the passages and the story “The Grammar Champions”, then answer the comprehension questions.
Work through Speaking, Writing and Activities in that order, recording the tasks that ask for your voice.
Finish with the quiz checkpoint and the self-assessment to see what you have learned.

visual.alt: Learners collaborating with books and school supplies

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person who helps us learn new things.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: My teacher helps me with my mathematics homework.
practiceSentences: Our teacher meets the parents in the hall on Friday.
The teacher wrote today's date on the board.
Did the teacher collect the reading journals yet?
Nora said, "Our teacher tells the best stories."
My teacher helped me sound out a tricky name.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: t - e - a - c - h - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Sit with an adult and ask the tutor how a teacher and a coach help people in different ways.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person who writes books or stories.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The author wrote a funny book about a talking camel.
practiceSentences: The library keeps every book by that author on one shelf.
An author plans a story before writing it.
Did the author draw the pictures too?
Adam said, "The author is visiting our school soon."
I wrote to an author, and she wrote back!
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: a - u - t - h - o - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult nearby, ask the tutor to name three jobs an author does before a book is printed.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A place where you can find and borrow books.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I go to the library to read and borrow books.
practiceSentences: The town library opens early on Saturday morning for children.
Quiet voices keep the library calm for readers.
May we visit the library after mathematics?
Sami said, "I found a space book in the library."
My library card is blue with gold stars.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: l - i - b - r - a - r - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask an adult to listen while the tutor explains the difference between borrowing and buying a book.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To finish school or a class.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: My sister will graduate from primary school next year.
practiceSentences: Older learners graduate in a small ceremony at the end of term.
Students graduate after finishing all their classes.
Did your cousin graduate from teaching college?
Mum said, "You will graduate before you know it."
I clapped when I watched my brother graduate.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: g - r - a - d - u - a - t - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult beside you, ask the tutor what happens on the day older learners graduate.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The subject where we work with numbers, shapes, and measuring.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: In mathematics, we learn to add and subtract.
practiceSentences: Our mathematics lesson comes straight after the morning register.
We solved tricky puzzles in mathematics this morning.
Is mathematics your favourite subject after English?
Maya said, "Mathematics helps me share sweets fairly."
I practise mathematics with my dad after dinner.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: m - a - t - h - e - m - a - t - i - c - s
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask an adult to sit with you, then ask the tutor for two mathematics words we also use in English.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The rules for how we put words together to speak and write.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We learn grammar so we can write correctly.
practiceSentences: The grammar contest tested verbs, questions, and small joining words.
Good grammar helps our sentences make sense.
Did you finish the grammar exercise on verbs?
The teacher said, "Check your grammar before handing in."
I am getting better at grammar every week.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: g - r - a - m - m - a - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, ask the tutor to fix one wrong sentence and explain which grammar rule helped.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A time when we learn something new in school.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Today's lesson is about authors and the books they write.
practiceSentences: A short lesson about prepositions can still teach us plenty.
Today's lesson taught us how to write questions.
Which lesson do you enjoy most on Mondays?
Theo said, "The science lesson was about plants."
My favourite lesson this week was about volcanoes.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: l - e - s - s - o - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask an adult to stay nearby while you tell the tutor about your favourite lesson this week.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Learning about things at school or at home.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Education helps us learn something new every day.
practiceSentences: Reading at home is an important part of my education.
A good education opens many doors in life.
Why is education important for every child?
Grandpa said, "Education is a treasure nobody can steal."
My parents work hard to support my education.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: e - d - u - c - a - t - i - o - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult listening, ask the tutor why education carries on outside the school gates.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To look at something carefully to learn about it.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I study my spelling words every night.
practiceSentences: We study together in the library when the classroom is busy.
We study spelling patterns every Tuesday morning.
Where do you study best, home or school?
Leo said, "Let us study for the quiz together."
I study my times tables before bed.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask an adult to sit beside you and ask the tutor for two clever ways to study spelling words.

dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We prepare for the test by reading.
practiceSentences: Groups prepare their posters before the contest begins on Friday.
The class helped prepare the room for open day.
How do you prepare for a spelling test?
Mum said, "Prepare your bag the night before school."
I prepare my pencils and books every evening.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: p - r - e - p - a - r - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult nearby, ask the tutor to help you plan how to prepare for a class contest.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A game or test to see who is best.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We had a spelling contest at school.
practiceSentences: The whole class cheered when the grammar contest was announced.
The reading contest starts after morning assembly.
Who won the handwriting contest last term?
Sami said, "I entered the poetry contest this year."
My team practised hard for the quiz contest.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - o - n - t - e - s - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask an adult to join you and ask the tutor to invent fair rules for a class contest.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A short paper or talk that tells about something.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I wrote a report about my favourite animal.
practiceSentences: A good report has a title, some facts, and a closing line.
Maya's report about camels earned a gold star.
Is your report about birds finished yet?
The teacher said, "Add a title to your report."
I read my report aloud to my family.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: r - e - p - o - r - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult beside you, ask the tutor which three parts every short report should have.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The subject or idea we talk or write about.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The topic of our story is animals.
practiceSentences: Choosing a clear topic makes the writing much easier to plan.
Our writing topic today is helping our neighbours.
Which topic did you choose for your report?
Adam said, "My topic is life by the sea."
I picked rain as my topic for the poster.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask an adult to listen while the tutor helps you narrow a wide topic into a small one.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Small pieces of information about something.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I added more details to my report.
practiceSentences: Small details, like the colour of the chalk, help readers picture the scene.
Good details make a story easy to picture.
Can you add more details about the market?
The teacher said, "Details bring your writing to life."
I noticed tiny details in the picture book.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: d - e - t - a - i - l - s
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, ask the tutor to add two details to a plain sentence you wrote.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Things we use at school or home, like pencils and paper.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We keep our pencils in the supply box.
practiceSentences: The cupboard holds a supply of glue, tape, and coloured paper.
Each table has a supply of paper and glue.
Is there a supply of crayons in the cupboard?
Theo said, "Our art supply box needs new paint."
I tidy the class supply shelf on Fridays.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - u - p - p - l - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask an adult to sit with you and ask the tutor to sort school supplies into two useful groups.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A tool used to clean pencil marks from paper.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I used my eraser to fix my mistake.
practiceSentences: A tidy pencil case holds a pencil, a ruler, and an eraser.
A soft eraser removes pencil marks without tearing.
May I borrow your eraser for a moment?
Mina said, "My eraser is shaped like a star."
I keep an eraser in my pencil case.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: e - r - a - s - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult nearby, ask the tutor why writers use an eraser instead of scribbling out mistakes.

rubrics.level4: Uses target words accurately and naturally

rubrics.level1: Capital letters and punctuation need support

outcomes.learningOutcome: Name the people, places, and supplies at school, using words like teacher, library, and eraser.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: All sixteen Word Wall words ticked in Activities 5, plus four school words used correctly in the Writing 1 sentences.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Talk about your school subjects, such as mathematics and grammar, and say which you prefer.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: A recorded Speaking 1 turn that names one subject, one supply and one reason, judged against the speaking rubric.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Tell about things that happened before, using the simple past tense (studied, prepared, went).
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Four correct past-tense forms in Activities 1, including the two irregular verbs, and three past-tense sentences in Writing 3.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Ask past-tense questions with Did, Was, Were and Wh– words, and answer them in full sentences.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Three correctly formed questions in Activities 3 and a recorded Speaking 2 exchange where did is followed by the base verb.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Show who owns something, using the possessive ’s (the teacher’s pen).
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Four correct possessive forms in the Grammar 6 practice and one accurate possessive caption in Writing 5.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Give reasons using the word because, joining a main idea to its reason.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Three joined sentences in Activities 4 and one spoken reason in Speaking 5 that answers the question why.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Prepare for a contest and write a short report about a topic you have chosen.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: A three-to-four sentence report in Writing 2 with a named topic, two details and a closing reason, revised after Writing 6.

readings.title: A Day at School
readings.setting: A primary school classroom, its library, and the learners' homes
readings.passageScript: My name is Adam. I go to school every morning. First, we have a mathematics lesson. I like numbers, but grammar is my favourite subject because it helps me write well. My teacher, Teacher Yasmin, is kind. She helps us study new words. At break time, I go to the library to read. Education is important to my family. One day, I want to graduate and become a teacher too!

readings.title: Getting Ready for the Contest
readings.setting: A primary school classroom, its library, and the learners' homes
readings.passageScript: Last week, our class had a spelling contest. My group wanted to win, so we made a good plan. We went to the library. We studied our words twice each day. We checked every letter with a friend. On the day of the contest, we felt ready. We did not win first place, but we were proud. We learned that hard work and teamwork help us do our best.

readings.title: In the Classroom
readings.setting: A primary school classroom, its library, and the learners' homes
readings.passageScript: Teacher Yasmin: “Good morning, class. Today’s lesson is about authors. Did you bring your books?” Maya: “Yes, Teacher Yasmin! I brought my book and my eraser.” Teacher Yasmin: “Very good, Maya. What did you read at home?” Maya: “I read a story about a clever camel. The author is very funny!”

readings.title: A Learning Song
readings.setting: A primary school classroom, its library, and the learners' homes
readings.passageScript:
Sing along:
“I studied, I studied, I studied today! I read my report and I put it away. In the library, quiet and bright, I learn something new — morning, noon, and night!”

readings.title: The Grammar Champions
readings.setting: A primary school classroom, its library, and the learners' homes
readings.passageScript:
Amal sat quietly in her classroom on a bright Monday morning. She held her pencil in one hand and her eraser in the other. Her teacher had just said something exciting.
“Next Friday,” said Teacher Yasmin, “we will have a grammar contest!”
The whole class cheered.
“You will work in groups,” she explained. “You will read, write, and even perform a short lesson. Start to prepare today!”
Amal joined her group with her friends Daniel and Nora. Their group name was “The Grammar Champions.”
“We need to choose a topic,” said Daniel.
“Let us pick something from the grammar book,” said Nora.
“What about prepositions?” asked Amal. “We can show how to use small words like ‘in’, ‘on’, and ‘under’.”
“I like it,” said Daniel. “It is simple, but important.”
The next day, the group met in the library. They wanted to study together and write their report.
Nora opened a book and smiled. “This author is funny,” she said. “He explains grammar with cartoons!”
Amal’s eyes lit up. “That gives me an idea,” she said. “We can draw pictures too.”
Daniel brought a notebook full of details. “These are notes from my sister’s class,” he said. “She already graduated from primary school.”
“She must be clever,” said Nora.
“She is,” said Daniel. “She loves education.”
For the next few days, the group worked hard. They practised their lesson in front of the class. They wrote their report about grammar rules. They checked every spelling word twice. They even collected classroom supplies like coloured chalk, tape, and posters.
Finally, it was contest day. The classroom had bright decorations and rows of chairs for visitors.
One of the judges asked, “Did you study well?”
“Yes, we did!” said Amal.
Another judge smiled. “What did you learn in mathematics today?”
“About shapes,” said Daniel. “But grammar is our focus today!”
When it was their turn, the Grammar Champions stood up tall.
Daniel began. “This topic is from our grammar book.”
Nora added, “We will show how to use the words ‘in’, ‘on’, and ‘under’.”
Amal stepped forward and pointed to the board. “The teacher’s pen is ‘on’ the desk,” she said. “The bag is ‘under’ the table.”
Daniel drew a funny cartoon on the board. Everyone laughed and clapped.
After the show, the judges talked quietly. Then Teacher Yasmin stood up and announced, “The winners are… The Grammar Champions!”
The class cheered again.
“We won!” whispered Amal. “All that preparing helped us.”
Later, the teacher asked, “Where did the contest happen?”
“In our own classroom,” Nora answered.
“And why did we win?” asked Daniel.
“Because we worked as a team,” said Amal. “And we gave a clear lesson with good details.”
At home, Amal told her mother the good news. “I feel so proud,” she said. “I want to learn even more grammar.”
Her mother hugged her. “That,” she said, “is the spirit of education.”
The next morning, Amal wrote in her journal:
I studied my grammar, I wrote a report,
I used my eraser to fix and to sort.
In the library, I read with my friends,
And now I have learned how good learning ends —
With joy, and with help, and a clever plan.
We’re Grammar Champions. Yes, we can!

comprehension.question: Which subject does Adam like best, and what reason does he give?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Adam likes grammar best because it helps him write well.
comprehension.explanation: Adam names grammar as his favourite subject and gives his reason straight after the word because.

comprehension.question: Name two things the group did to prepare for the spelling contest.
comprehension.correctAnswer: They went to the library, studied their words twice each day, and checked every letter with a friend; any two of these are correct.
comprehension.explanation: The middle of the passage lists three preparation steps, so any two of them earn the mark.

comprehension.question: What is today’s lesson about in Teacher Yasmin’s classroom?
comprehension.explanation: Teacher Yasmin says it in her opening line, before she asks about the books.

comprehension.question: In the song, where does the singer go to learn something new?
comprehension.correctAnswer: The singer learns in the library, which the song calls quiet and bright.
comprehension.explanation: The third line of the song names the library and describes what it is like.

comprehension.question: What is the name of Amal’s group, and which topic did they choose?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Her group is called The Grammar Champions, and they chose prepositions.
comprehension.explanation: The group name appears when Amal joins Daniel and Nora, and Amal suggests prepositions a few lines later.

comprehension.question: Where does Adam go at break time, and what does he do there?
comprehension.correctAnswer: He goes to the library at break time so that he can read.
comprehension.explanation: The sentence beginning At break time gives both the place and the reason.

comprehension.question: The group did not win first place. How did they feel, and why did they feel that way?
comprehension.correctAnswer: They felt proud because their hard work and teamwork helped them do their best.
comprehension.explanation: The final two sentences give the feeling first and then the reason behind it.

comprehension.question: Which two things did Maya bring to school that morning?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Maya brought her book and her eraser.
comprehension.explanation: Maya lists both items when she answers Teacher Yasmin’s first question.

comprehension.question: Which word in the song tells you that the singing happened in the past?
comprehension.correctAnswer: The word studied tells us that it happened in the past.
comprehension.explanation: Studied ends in –ed, the regular past-tense ending taught in Grammar 1.

comprehension.question: Why did the Grammar Champions win? Give two reasons from the story.
comprehension.correctAnswer: They worked as a team, and they gave a clear lesson with good details.
comprehension.explanation: Amal gives both reasons herself when the teacher asks why the group won.

comprehension.question: Adam wants to graduate and become a teacher. What does that tell you about how he feels about school?
comprehension.correctAnswer: It shows that he values school and enjoys learning enough to want to teach others.
comprehension.explanation: The passage never states his feelings, so the answer is inferred from his wish to return as a teacher.

comprehension.question: Find two past-tense verbs in “Getting Ready for the Contest” and write them down.
comprehension.correctAnswer: Any two of: had, wanted, made, went, studied, checked, felt, learned.
comprehension.explanation: Each of these verbs tells about last week, so each one is in the past tense.

grammar.title: The Simple Past Tense
grammar.explanation: We use the simple past tense to talk about things that already happened — yesterday, last week, or a long time ago. It helps us tell stories and say what we did.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Most verbs add –ed: study → studied, play → played, jump → jumped.
Some verbs are special (irregular): go → went, have → had, eat → ate.
Time words help, like yesterday, last week, or two days ago.
Example: “The student studied for the grammar contest.”
grammar.commonMistake: Mistake: “He goed to the park.” Fix: go is one of the special verbs, so we say went — “He went to the park.”
grammar.memoryTip: Think of the past tense as a time capsule. When something happened before now, open the capsule and use the past form.
grammar.practice: Write the past tense of the verb in brackets. | Yesterday, I ______ (visit) my grandmother. | They ______ (play) football last week. | We ______ (eat) rice for dinner. | She ______ (prepare) her report last night.

grammar.title: Yes/No Questions in the Past
grammar.explanation: These questions ask whether something happened before now, and the answer is simply yes or no. They keep a conversation moving.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
With most verbs, use Did + subject + the base verb: “Did you eat?”
To ask how someone or something was, use Was or Were + subject: “Was she happy?”
Use was with I, he, she and it. Use were with you, we and they.
Example: “Did you forget your eraser?” · “Was the author at the lesson?”
grammar.commonMistake: Mistake: “Did he went to the store?” Fix: after did we use the base verb, with no –ed — “Did he go to the store?”
grammar.memoryTip: Did brings the past with it, so the verb after it can relax and stay in its plain base form.
grammar.practice: Fill in the blank with Did, Was, or Were. | ______ you see the film last week? | ______ she at the contest? | ______ they play outside yesterday? | ______ it cold last night?

grammar.title: Wh– Questions in the Past
grammar.explanation: Wh– questions begin with what, where, when, who, why or how, and they ask for details about something that already happened.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Use this order: Wh– word + did + subject + base verb.
Example: “What did you learn in mathematics?”
Example: “Where did the contest happen?”
Special case: when who is the subject, we leave did out — “Who visited the library?”
grammar.commonMistake: Mistake: “What did you learned?” Fix: keep the base verb after did — “What did you learn?”
grammar.memoryTip: Picture did as a helper standing between the question word and the verb, carrying the past tense for both of them.
grammar.practice: Complete each question with the correct Wh– word (What, When, or Where). | ______ did you eat for lunch? | ______ did the lesson start? | ______ did you go on the school trip?

grammar.title: Using ‘from’ to Show Where Something Comes From
grammar.explanation: The little word from points back to the place, book or person that something came out of, which makes our information clear.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Use this order: subject + verb + from + the origin.
Example: “This topic is from our grammar book.”
You can also say where a person comes from: “I come from a town beside the river.”
grammar.commonMistake: Mistake: “This book is our grammar book.” The word from is missing. Fix: “This idea is from our grammar book.”
grammar.memoryTip: Think of from as a small bridge that joins a thing to the place it travelled out of.
grammar.practice: Fill in each blank with from. | This song is ______ a popular singer. | The homework is ______ our last lesson. | My friend is ______ the next town.

grammar.title: Present Tense for Customs and Statements
grammar.explanation: We use the present tense for things that happen again and again, like everyday habits, and for facts that stay true.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
With I, you, we and they, use the base verb: “They play.” · “Teachers give lessons every morning.”
With he, she and it, add –s or –es: “He plays.” · “She goes to school.”
grammar.commonMistake: Mistake: “He play football.” Fix: he, she and it always want the extra letter — “He plays football.”
grammar.memoryTip: Say the little rhyme to yourself: “He, she, it — add an –s to it!”
grammar.practice: Write the correct present-tense form of the verb in brackets. | My dad ______ (cook) breakfast every day. | The students ______ (read) books in class. | She ______ (play) with her friends on weekends. | They ______ (study) grammar every morning.

grammar.title: The Possessive ’s and the Conjunction ‘because’
grammar.explanation: The possessive ’s shows that something belongs to somebody, and because joins an idea to the reason behind it.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
For one owner, add ’s to the name: student → student’s, teacher → teacher’s.
Put the owner in front of the thing: “the student’s report,” “the teacher’s lesson.”
If the owner is plural and already ends in –s, add only an apostrophe: “the students’ reports.”
For a reason, put because between the two ideas: “I like the library because it is quiet.”
grammar.commonMistake: Mistake: “The teachers lesson was about authors.” The apostrophe is missing, so fix it to “The teacher’s lesson was about authors.” Mistake: “I like the library it is quiet because.” Fix: “I like the library because it is quiet.”
grammar.memoryTip: Find the owner and stick the little ’s on. Then use because as the bridge that answers the question why.
grammar.practice: Write the correct possessive form of the owner in brackets. | The ______ (cat) toy is missing. | The ______ (teacher) desk is tidy. | My ______ (sister) book is on the table. | The ______ (child) drawing is on the wall. | Now finish each sentence with your own reason, using because. | I love reading ______. | We went to the library ______. | I enjoy grammar ______.

speaking.title: Speaking 1 — Talk About Your School Day
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Get ready to say three sentences about your day at school:
“Today I studied ______.”
“My favourite subject is ______ because ______.”
“At school I use a ______.” (name a supply)
Record yourself. Check: Did you speak clearly? Did you use the word because? Did you name a subject and a supply?
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult sitting beside you, read your three school-day sentences to the tutor and ask which one sounded clearest.

speaking.title: Speaking 2 — Ask and Answer About the Past
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Practise asking a partner or your AI tutor these questions, then answer them:
“What did you study yesterday?”
“Did you go to the library this week?”
“Where did you do your homework?”
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Ask an adult to stay in the room, then ask the tutor to check that did is followed by the base verb in each of your questions.

speaking.title: Speaking 3 — Guess My Subject
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Play with your AI tutor or your family:
Think of a school subject, but do not say it.
Give a clue: “In this subject, we work with numbers.”
The other person guesses: “Is it mathematics?” Say “Yes, it is!” or “No, try again.” Then swap turns.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult playing too, ask the tutor to give you three clues and let you guess the subject before you swap roles.

speaking.title: Speaking 4 — Vocabulary Expert Interview
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Choose six Unit 2 words from your Word Wall, such as report, supply, and graduate.
Ask an adult to interview you: “What does the word report mean?”
Answer in one full sentence: “A report is a short talk or paper that tells about something.”
Then swap over and interview the adult about three more words.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Ask an adult to listen in, then ask the tutor to interview you about four school words and score each answer out of two.

speaking.title: Speaking 5 — Explain and Respond
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Explain one idea from this unit to an adult, such as why the library is a good place to study.
Give your reason with because: “I study in the library because it is quiet.”
Then answer two follow-up questions, such as “What did you study there last week?”
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, ask the tutor to challenge your reason politely and to check that you answered with a because sentence.

speaking.title: Speaking 6 — One-Minute Unit Talk
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Plan a one-minute talk called “How I prepare for a contest.”
Open with one sentence, give three connected details, and close by saying what you learned.
Record the talk, listen back with an adult, and name one thing you would improve next time.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Ask an adult to time you, then ask the tutor to comment on your opening sentence, your three details, and your closing line.

writing.title: Writing 1 — Sentences About School
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Finish these sentences, then write two more of your own:
Today the teacher gave a lesson about ______.
I like the library because ______.
Yesterday I studied ______.
This is my ______. (Write one sentence using the possessive ’s, like “This is Nora’s eraser.”)
writing.modelText: Today the teacher gave a lesson about authors. I like the library because it is quiet and full of stories. Yesterday I studied my spelling words twice. This is Nora’s eraser, and this one is mine.
writing.sentenceStarter: Today the teacher gave a lesson about
writing.successCriteria: I finished all four sentences; I used one school word in each; I used the possessive ’s once; I checked my capital letters and full stops
writing.support: Say each sentence aloud before you write it, and keep your Word Wall open beside you.
writing.extension: Write two extra sentences about a lesson you would like to teach the class.

writing.title: Writing 2 — A Short Report
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write a short report of three or four sentences about a topic you know well, such as an animal, a game, or your town.
Name your topic, add two details, and finish with one sentence about why you like it.
Then draw a small picture to go beside your report.
writing.modelText: Camels are strong animals that live in dry places. They can walk for days without drinking water. Their wide feet stop them sinking into soft sand. I like camels because they carry heavy loads without complaining.
writing.successCriteria: I named my topic in the first sentence; I gave two true details; I ended with a reason using because; my picture matches my report
writing.support: Choose a topic you could already talk about for one minute, then write those spoken ideas down.
writing.extension: Add a heading and one interesting fact that most readers would not know.

writing.title: Writing 3 — First, I Prepared
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write three sentences about a time you prepared for something at school.
Begin the first sentence with “First, I ______.”
Use the simple past tense in all three sentences, and check every verb ending.
writing.modelText: First, I collected my spelling list and a sharp pencil. Then I studied five words each evening with my sister. On the morning of the test I felt calm because I prepared carefully.
writing.successCriteria: I began with First; every verb tells about the past; I spelled the –ed endings correctly; my sentences follow a sensible order
writing.support: List the three steps in order before you write, using one word for each step.
writing.extension: Add a fourth sentence beginning Later that day to link your steps together.

writing.title: Writing 4 — Helping My Team
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Imagine you joined the Grammar Champions for the contest.
Write one sentence about the job you would do to help your team.
Then write a second sentence that gives your reason, using because.
writing.modelText: I would draw the pictures for our poster so the whole class can see each rule. I chose that job because I like drawing and it makes our lesson easy to follow.
writing.expectedLength: 2 sentences, one of them a reason
writing.successCriteria: I named one clear job; my second sentence uses because; both sentences make sense together; I checked my punctuation
writing.support: Think of three jobs the group needed doing, then pick the one you would enjoy most.
writing.extension: Write one sentence about the job you would find hardest, and explain why.

writing.title: Writing 5 — Draw and Caption the Winners
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Draw Amal, Daniel, and Nora holding their contest prize.
Write one sentence under your picture about how the three friends feel.
Use the possessive ’s once, for example “Amal’s smile is the widest.”
writing.modelText: Amal, Daniel, and Nora feel proud because their hard work paid off. Nora’s hands shake a little as she holds the prize.
writing.successCriteria: My drawing shows all three friends; my caption names a feeling; I used the possessive ’s correctly; the caption sits under the picture
writing.support: Choose the feeling word first, then build the caption around it.
writing.extension: Add a speech bubble for one character, punctuated with speech marks.

writing.title: Writing 6 — Check and Improve Your Report
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Read your short report aloud to an adult and listen for any sentence that sounds wrong.
Check every capital letter, every full stop, and every past-tense verb ending.
Rewrite two sentences to make them clearer, then say what you improved.
writing.modelText: I changed “We goed to the library” to “We went to the library.” I also added a full stop and one more detail about the quiet reading corner.
writing.successCriteria: I read my work aloud; I found at least two things to fix; my rewrites are clearer than before; I explained one improvement
writing.support: Read one sentence at a time and put your finger on each full stop as you reach it.
writing.extension: Swap reports with a partner and suggest one improvement each, kindly and clearly.

activities.title: Activities 1 — Past Tense (Fill in the Blank)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write the past tense of the verb in brackets.
1. Yesterday, I ______ (study) for the contest.
2. She ______ (prepare) her report last night.
3. We ______ (go) to the library on Monday.
4. They ______ (read) a book by a funny author.
activities.answerSummary: studied, prepared, went, read — the last two are irregular verbs that do not simply add –ed.

activities.title: Activities 2 — Word Match
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Match each word to its meaning and write the correct letter.
1. eraser 2. library 3. teacher 4. contest
a. a place with many books to read or borrow
b. a game or test to see who is best
c. a tool that cleans pencil marks
d. a person who helps us learn
activities.answerSummary: c, a, d, b — the two people words are teacher and author, so check that the learner keeps them apart.

activities.title: Activities 3 — Ask the Question (Wh– Questions in the Past)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write the question that fits each answer.
1. ______? → “I studied mathematics.”
2. ______? → “The contest happened in our classroom.”
3. ______? → “I went to the library because it is quiet.”
activities.answerSummary: What did you study? Where did the contest happen? Why did you go to the library? Wording may vary a little.

activities.title: Activities 4 — Because Sentences
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Join the two ideas with the word because.
1. I like grammar. It helps me write well.
2. We went to the library. We wanted to study.
3. Amal felt proud. Her group won the contest.
activities.answerSummary: Each answer keeps the main idea first and puts the reason after because, with one full stop at the end.

activities.title: Activities 5 — Word Wall Check
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Sort the sixteen Unit 2 words into the three Word Wall groups: people and places, subjects and learning, doing school work.
Tick each word you can read, say, and use in a sentence.
Write one sentence explaining why supply and eraser belong together.
activities.answerSummary: Four words go in group one, six in group two, and six in group three, matching the Word Wall in the vocabulary book.

activities.title: Activities 6 — Find the Words in the Story
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Read “The Grammar Champions” again and hunt for these ten words: contest, eraser, topic, library, study, report, author, details, graduate, supplies.
Copy out the sentence each word appears in.
Underline the word, then say the sentence aloud to an adult.
activities.answerSummary: All ten words really do appear in the story, so every hunt should end with ten copied sentences.

assignments.title: School and Learning learning portfolio
assignments.instructions:
Submit two pieces for your School and Learning portfolio: Writing 2 — A Short Report, and a recording of Speaking 6 — One-Minute Unit Talk.
Improve both pieces first, using the Grade 3 writing and speaking rubrics.
Marking guidance for the teacher: award the sixteen writing marks for a named topic, two accurate details, correct past-tense verb forms, and one reason joined with because. Award the sixteen speaking marks for a clear opening sentence, three connected details, an audible closing line, and at least four Unit 2 words used correctly. Where a criterion is only partly met, award the level that matches the strongest evidence in the whole piece.
assignments.sourceFile: Derived from unit writing and speaking tasks

quizzes.question: Which word means a place where you can find and borrow books?
quizzes.explanation: A library is a place, while an eraser is a tool, a contest is an event, and mathematics is a subject.

quizzes.question: What does the word ‘author’ mean?
quizzes.options: A tool used to clean pencil marks from paper. | A short paper or talk that tells about something. | Things we use at school or home, like pencils and paper. | A person who writes books or stories.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A person who writes books or stories.
quizzes.explanation: The other three options are the meanings of eraser, report and supply.

quizzes.question: In ‘A Day at School’, why does Adam say grammar is his favourite subject?
quizzes.options: Because it helps him write well. | Because his sister teaches it. | Because it happens after lunch every single day. | Because he likes counting numbers.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Because it helps him write well.
quizzes.explanation: Adam gives that exact reason right after the word because in the passage.

quizzes.question: Which sentence uses the simple past tense correctly?
quizzes.options: She prepare her report last night. | He goed to the library on Monday morning. | We studied our spelling words twice. | They eats rice for dinner yesterday.
quizzes.correctAnswer: We studied our spelling words twice.
quizzes.explanation: Study is a regular verb, so it adds –ed; the others need prepared, went and ate.

quizzes.question: Which question correctly asks about something that happened in the past?
quizzes.options: What did you learned in class? | Where did the contest happen? | Did he went to the library yesterday? | When you did start the lesson?
quizzes.explanation: After did we keep the base verb, and did must come before the subject, not after it.

quizzes.question: Which sentence shows the possessive ’s used correctly?
quizzes.options: The teacher’s desk is tidy. | The teachers desk is tidy. | The teacher desk’s is tidy. | The teachers’s desk is tidy.
quizzes.explanation: One owner takes an apostrophe and then s, attached to the owner rather than to the thing owned.

quizzes.question: Which word joins these two ideas: “I like the library ______ it is quiet.”?
quizzes.options: but | so that | because | even though
quizzes.explanation: Because introduces a reason; but shows contrast, so that shows purpose, and even though shows surprise.

quizzes.question: In ‘The Grammar Champions’, where did Amal’s group go to study and write their report?
quizzes.options: To the market | To the sports hall | To Teacher Yasmin’s office | To the school library
quizzes.explanation: The story says the group met in the library the next day to study together and write their report.

quizzes.question: Which word means small pieces of information about something?
quizzes.explanation: Supply names objects, topic names the idea you write about, and education names learning itself.

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word: We ______ for the contest by reading and practising every day.
quizzes.explanation: Prepare means to get ready, and the –d ending puts it in the past like the rest of the sentence.

liveSessions.title: School and Learning: Launch and the Word Wall
liveSessions.beforeSession: Read the unit overview and learn the four words in Group 1.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min Word Wall retrieval; 10 min teacher models teacher, author, library and graduate; 15 min paired meaning match; 10 min building sentences with the new words; 5 min exit question
liveSessions.afterSession: Add three Group 1 words to your own word list and use each one in writing.

liveSessions.title: School and Learning: Reading ‘A Day at School’
liveSessions.beforeSession: Read ‘A Day at School’ once quietly and once aloud.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min prediction from the title; 10 min shared reading with expression; 15 min scanning for answers; 10 min partner retell; 5 min review of tricky words
liveSessions.afterSession: Reread the passage to an adult and answer the three comprehension questions.

liveSessions.title: School and Learning: Past-tense grammar workshop
liveSessions.beforeSession: Complete Grammar 1 and bring your four past-tense answers.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min verb warm-up; 10 min teacher models regular and irregular past forms; 15 min guided correction of wrong sentences; 10 min partner quiz using Did and Was; 5 min feedback
liveSessions.afterSession: Rewrite two sentences from your report in the simple past tense.

liveSessions.title: School and Learning: Asking and answering about the past
liveSessions.beforeSession: Prepare three questions you would like to ask about last week.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min question chant; 10 min teacher models Wh– question order; 15 min interview circles; 10 min Guess My Subject in pairs; 5 min reflection on clear speaking
liveSessions.afterSession: Record Speaking 2 and listen back for the word did in every question.

liveSessions.title: School and Learning: Report writing conference
liveSessions.beforeSession: Bring your draft report and a coloured pencil for editing.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min sharing of chosen topics; 10 min teacher models adding details; 15 min independent drafting; 10 min partner feedback using the writing rubric; 5 min next-step targets
liveSessions.afterSession: Improve one part of your report using the feedback you were given.

liveSessions.title: School and Learning: Contest performance and review
liveSessions.beforeSession: Rehearse your one-minute talk twice and time yourself.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min settling and contest rules; 10 min group performances; 15 min class grammar contest; 10 min quiz checkpoint; 5 min self-assessment
liveSessions.afterSession: Complete the unit quiz and tick your self-assessment statements.

teacherNotes.note: Model every new word aloud and check meaning before independent work. Mix spoken, written and drawn responses so each learner can show what they know. Whenever the AI tutor is used, an adult stays in the room; no child works alone with the device. Give feedback on complete sentences, text evidence and precise vocabulary.

Suggested tutor prompts, grouped by section.
Vocabulary: “Can you quiz me on the Group 1 words?” · “What is the difference between a teacher and an author?” · “Why is education important?”
Grammar: “Can you check my grammar answers?” · “Why do we say ‘Did you go?’ and not ‘Did you went?’” · “Can you give me five more sentences to practise the past tense?”
Reading and listening: “Can you read ‘A Day at School’ with me and explain any hard words?” · “Can you play a listening game about the past tense?”
Speaking: “Can you ask me past-tense questions and check my answers?” · “Can you play ‘Guess My Subject’ with me?”
Writing: “Can you check my past-tense sentences and tell me what to fix?” · “Can you help me add more details to my report?”
An adult sits with the learner for every tutor session and closes the session at the end.

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: 1. studied 2. prepared 3. went 4. read. Items 3 and 4 are irregular: go becomes went, and read keeps its spelling while changing sound.
1. c 2. a 3. d 4. b. If a learner swaps teacher and author, ask who explains the book and who wrote it.
1. What did you study? 2. Where did the contest happen? 3. Why did you go to the library? Accept any Wh– word that fits, provided did is followed by the base verb.
1. I like grammar because it helps me write well. 2. We went to the library because we wanted to study. 3. Amal felt proud because her group won the contest.

People and places: teacher, author, library, graduate. Subjects and learning: mathematics, grammar, lesson, education, study, prepare. Doing school work: contest, report, topic, details, supply, eraser. Supply and eraser sit together because both name things we use to do school work.
Every one of the ten words appears in “The Grammar Champions”. Check that each copied sentence is complete and punctuated, and that the underlined word matches the hunt list.
library. A library is a place; an eraser is a tool, a contest is an event, and mathematics is a subject.
A person who writes books or stories. The three wrong options define eraser, report and supply instead.
Because it helps him write well. Adam states that reason himself in ‘A Day at School’.
We studied our spelling words twice. Ask the learner to correct the other three: prepared, went and ate.
Where did the contest happen? Point out that did carries the past, so the verb stays in its base form.
The teacher’s desk is tidy. The apostrophe and s attach to the owner, never to the thing that is owned.
because. It introduces the reason; but, so that and even though each change the meaning of the sentence.
To the school library. The story says the group met there the next day to study and write their report.
details. Contrast it with supply, which names objects, and topic, which names the idea being written about.
prepared. The sentence tells about the past, so prepare takes the –d ending taught in Grammar 1.

selfAssessment.statement: I can name the people, places, and supplies at school.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can talk about my school subjects and say which one I like best.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can use the simple past tense to say what I did.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can ask past-tense questions, such as “Did you study?” and “What did you learn?”
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can use the possessive ’s to show who owns something.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can give a reason with the word because.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself
